query: Plastic carrier size 40-90 gutta percha composition

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Gutta-percha is the most commonly used core obturation material in modern endodontics. [25] They are manufactured as cones comprised mostly of zinc oxide and gutta-percha with added plasticizers and radiopacifiers. [5] Gutta-percha is a naturally occurring thermoplastic material that originates from specific trees in the Malaysian region. [25] The popularity of gutta-percha as an obturation material stems from its variable chemical composition. Gutta-percha exists in 2 crystalline phases, alpha and beta, and can cycle between these phases when heated is applied or removed. The alpha phase of gutta-percha occurs when heated, producing a tacky material that can flow under pressure. Alternatively, the beta phase is when the gutta-percha is unheated, forming a solid mass of compactable material. [1] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Gutta-percha has many advantages, such as ease of manipulation, radiopacity, low toxicity, and plasticity. However, it does not bond to dentin and shrinks when cooled. [5] [25] Gutta-percha cones are available in standardized 0.02 taper as specified by the International Organization of Standardization and non-standardized sizes. [5] This allows clinicians to mechanically prepare the canal space using various commercial rotary systems using standardized file sizes. When the canal is prepared to a given file size, a gutta-percha cone of the same size can be inserted and adapted to the canal. [1] [5] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- Gutta Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Ease of manipulation Radiopaque Low toxicity and plasticity Relatively inexpensive [1] [5] [25] Disadvantages: No dentin-bonding properties Material shrinkage upon setting [1] [5] [25] Bioceramic Core Obturation Materials Advantages: Biocompatible Ability to promote biomineralization High fracture resistance Antimicrobial Dimensional stability upon setting [26] [27] Disadvantages: Technically difficult to manipulate Expensive Shorter shelf-life (cv other core materials) [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Other Core Materials
retrieved_docs.content: There are various other core obturation materials commercially available; however, no statistical difference in terms of reduction in leakage has been noted between them and gutta-percha. [1] [5] These materials include GI-impregnated gutta-percha cones and resin-based obturation systems. [30] Using a resin core in addition to a resin sealer is believed to allow the formation of a 'monoblock,' or bonded homogenous mass of material within the root canal. [31] However, this has not yet been achieved with current materials. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: Since there are numerous types of obturation materials, there are several ways to insert them into the root canal, and as a result, this section focuses primarily on the insertion technique of gutta-percha. Gutta-percha has 2 main obturation techniques, cold lateral condensation and warm vertical compaction. [5] [39] The cold lateral condensation technique involves the selection of a master gutta-percha cone, the use of spreaders, and the insertion of secondary gutta-percha cones. [5] [40] The master cone is a gutta-percha cone with a taper and diameter that matches the prepared root canal at the working length. This master cone is measured to the working length and inserted into the canal, where it displays slight resistance to removal, aptly referred to as "tug back" if the correct size cone is selected. [5] After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. At this point, a finger spreader or a hand spreader is pre-fit in the canal to reach within 1 to 2 mm short of the working length, and appropriately sized secondary gutta-percha cones are selected that closely match the size of the spreader. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: The warm vertical compaction technique uses a master gutta-percha cone, Schilder pluggers, and a heat source. [42] [40] [42] The master cone in this method should fit just short of the working length and have a slight tug back. After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. The master cone is reinserted, and the coronal portion is removed with a heated instrument. [42] Next, a heated plugger is used to gradually and sequentially remove portions of the coronal gutta-percha while softening the cone below. [42] The heated plugger is removed from the canal, a non-heated plugger is inserted, and the warm gutta-percha is compacted to force the now-plasticized material apically. [42] This process is repeated until 4 to 5 mm of adapted gutta-percha remains at the apex, forming an effective seal. [40] Small gutta-percha cones are inserted into the canal, heated with a heated plugger, and then compacted with a non-heated plugger. This is repeated until the canal is fully obturated with plasticized gutta-percha. [5] [42] [5] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: The master cone is inserted into the canal to the working length, followed by the spreader, which, if properly selected, should reach 2 mm shorter. The spreader is then rotated and withdrawn from the canal, and the matching gutta-percha cone is inserted into the vacant space. [39] The process is repeated until the operator cannot insert the spreader deeper than the coronal third of the prepared canal. The excess gutta-percha is seared off with a heated instrument and compacted using a root canal plugger. [5] [41] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: Core materials exist to fill the bulk of the root canal space and act as a plug to prevent any leakage or ingress of foreign material. They take up most of the area within the canal and rely on the sealer to fill any voids or irregular spaces between it and the canal walls. Although other materials have been fabricated recently, gutta-percha remains the most widely used and accepted core filling material. Properties of an ideal core obturation material: Ease of manipulation Dimensional stability Excellent sealing ability (apically and laterally) Non-irritating to periapical tissues Radiopaque (to be visible on radiographs) Non-staining or discoloring Antimicrobial Biocompatible [1] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Properties of an ideal sealer material: Ability to establish a fluid-tight seal Radiopaque (to be visible on radiographs) Antimicrobial Non-shrinking upon setting Non-staining Non-irritating to periapical tissues Insoluble in tissue fluid Biocompatible [1] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The applications of hydroxyapatite in hard tissue restoration and drug delivery do not typically involve its pure form. Pure hydroxyapatite has relatively low mechanical properties and is brittle, making it unsuitable for load-bearing applications. Consequently, hydroxyapatite is often incorporated into composites or polymers to enhance its functionality. [11] [16] In this case, the enhanced properties of hydroxyapatite result from the compressive strength of the hydroxyapatite ceramic phase, combined with the toughness and elasticity of the polymer or composite matrix. Generally, hydroxyapatite exhibits resistance to resorption in vivo at a rate of 1% to 2% per year, providing long-lasting structural support in the defect area. [8] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Advantages and Disadvantages -- ZOE Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages: Absorption, if extruded past the apex Anti-inflammatory effects Antimicrobial effects [5] [9] Disadvantages: Transition into a weak, porous state upon setting Shrinkage upon setting [5] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Structure of Set Cement
retrieved_docs.content: The cement consists of a core and matrix. The core constitutes unmixed powder particles, whereas the matrix comprises polyacid and HEMA polymer linked with hydrogen bonding.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonsurgical Rhinoplasty Using Fillers -- Equipment -- Filler Selection
retrieved_docs.content: Another pivotal filler attribute is the elastic coefficient, denoted as G-prime, which reflects the filler's resistance to deformation or stiffness. High G-prime fillers, such as CaHa, resist deformation, offering robust "filling" effects with less product. However, they may induce more postprocedural edema and discomfort and may feel less natural than low G-prime options like HA. Permanent fillers, such as silicone and polymethylmethacrylate, are best avoided due to the risk of granulomas and irreversible complications, including vascular injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Sugar‑Free cholestyramine resin 4000 MG Powder for Oral Suspension contains cholestyramine resin as an ingredient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Sugar‑Free cholestyramine resin 4000 MG Powder for Oral Suspension contains cholestyraminová pryskyřice as its active ingredient. Cholestyraminová pryskyřice is classified as an iontoměniče (ion‑exchange resin), reflecting its mechanism of binding bile acids in the gastrointestinal tract. Chemically, cholestyraminová pryskyřice has polystyreny as its parent polymer, indicating that its polymeric backbone is derived from polystyrene. Recognizing that the product’s key component is a polystyrene‑based ion‑exchange resin informs clinicians about its mode of action, therapeutic use, and potential drug‑interaction considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Fat emulsion is a type of dietary supplement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2410
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmaceutical combination cholestyramine resin / diclofenac contains cholestyraminová pryskyřice as a constituent part. Cholestyraminová pryskyřice is classified as a type of polystyreny, indicating that the resin component is derived from the polystyrene family of synthetic polymers. This relationship underscores that the resin element of cholestyramine resin / diclofenac is a polystyrene‑based anion‑exchange resin, which can affect its drug delivery characteristics and interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2072
retrieved_docs.content: Cholestyramine resin 4000 mg powder for oral suspension is formulated with methylated pectin as an inactive ingredient; methylated pectin serves as a non‑therapeutic component that provides viscosity, stability, or binding properties in the suspension, while the active cholestyramine resin functions as a bile‑acid sequestrant. The presence of methylated pectin does not contribute to the drug’s pharmacologic effect but may influence the product’s texture and patient acceptability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1631
retrieved_docs.content: Diclofenac is a key active ingredient incorporated into several combination pharmaceutical products. The “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product” and the “diclofenac / misoprostol Oral Product” both contain diclofenac as an active component, indicating that diclofenac is essential to the therapeutic effect of these misoprostol‑containing formulations. In the “cholestyramine resin / diclofenac” product, diclofenac is present as a part of the composite preparation, highlighting its role alongside cholestyramine resin. Additionally, the “diclofenac / misoprostol” combination is classified as a type of diclofenac formulation, underscoring its identity within the broader category of diclofenac‑based medicines. Together, these relationships illustrate how diclofenac functions as a central active ingredient and structural element across multiple combination drug products, informing clinicians about its presence and classification in various therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 85
retrieved_docs.content: the pharmacologically active component is anhydrous dextrose (the active moiety), while dextrose monohydrate is listed as the active ingredient; together these anhydrous and monohydrate forms constitute the essential active substance of the formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2642
retrieved_docs.content: Cholestyraminová pryskyřice (cholestyramine resin) is an ion‑exchange polymer that originates from a polystyrene base, making polystyreny its parent material. This resin functions as the active ingredient in the product Sugar‑Free cholestyramine resin 4000 MG Powder for Oral Suspension, providing bile‑acid binding activity for lipid‑lowering therapy. In addition, cholestyraminová pryskyřice is a constituent part of the combination formulation cholestyramine resin / diclofenac, linking its ion‑exchange properties with the anti‑inflammatory effects of diclofenac. Recognizing cholestyraminová pryskyřice as both an ion exchanger derived from polystyrene and a component of these specific pharmaceutical products informs clinicians about its therapeutic role and potential considerations in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2796
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are classified as a type of cholestyramine resin, indicating that they belong to the cholestyraminová pryskyřice family. Anion exchange resins (aniontoměniče) are also linked to cholestyramine resin as a parent class, showing that they share a common chemical lineage. Cholestyramine resin itself is a subclass of polystyrenes, meaning that the resin’s structure is based on a polystyrene polymer backbone. Together, these relationships highlight that both bile acid sequestrants and anion exchange resins are derived from cholestyramine resin, which in turn is built upon polystyrene chemistry, a fact that is important for understanding their pharmacologic properties, mechanisms of action, and potential interactions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1389
retrieved_docs.content: Sugar‑Free cholestyramine resin 4000 mg powder for oral suspension is formulated with cholestyramine resin as its active ingredient. Cholestyramine resin itself is a polymeric bile‑acid sequestrant that is derived from a polystyrene base, indicating that polystyrene serves as the parent material for this resin. In combination products such as cholestyramine resin/diclofenac, cholestyramine resin constitutes a distinct part of the formulation, providing its sequestrant properties alongside diclofenac. Within the presented classification scheme, antipruritic agents are grouped under the broader category of cholestyramine resin, suggesting a taxonomic relationship in which antipruritic preparations are considered a subclass of cholestyramine‑based compounds. This interconnected hierarchy highlights how cholestyramine resin functions both as a therapeutic ingredient in specific oral suspensions and as a structural parent for related drug categories and combination formulations.
